verses.text: Samuel calls Israel to witness against him: 'whose ox have I taken? whom have I defrauded?' Not one can accuse him. A lifetime of clean hands lets a leader speak with authority. Integrity is a treasure no office can buy and no enemy can take away.

Samuel calls Israel to witness against him: 'whose ox have I taken? whom have I defrauded?' Not one can accuse him. A lifetime of clean hands lets a leader speak with authority. Integrity is a treasure no office can buy and no enemy can take away.

Samuel rehearses the LORD's faithfulness — how He raised up Moses and Aaron and brought their fathers out of Egypt. Before he confronts their sin, he reminds them of grace. Remembering what God has done is the right ground for both repentance and renewed obedience.

Again and again, he says, they forgot the LORD and were oppressed; they cried out, and He sent deliverers and saved them. The pattern of their whole history is sin, suffering, crying out, and rescue. God's mercy keeps outlasting His people's forgetfulness.

Again and again, he says, they forgot the LORD and were oppressed; they cried out, and He sent deliverers and saved them. The pattern of their whole history is sin, suffering, crying out, and rescue. God's mercy keeps outlasting His people's forgetfulness.

Their demand for a king, when 'the LORD your God was your king,' was at heart distrust of God. Yet Samuel holds out hope: if both they and their king will fear and serve the LORD, it shall be well. God meets even our wrong choices with a path of obedience forward.

To seal his words, Samuel calls on the LORD, and in dry wheat harvest comes thunder and rain. The people 'greatly feared the LORD and Samuel.' A word backed by God's own power awes the heart. Sometimes He must thunder before we will listen and tremble.

Terrified, they beg Samuel to pray for them. He answers, 'Fear not... only fear the LORD,' and assures them, 'the LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake.' Our security rests not on our worthiness but on God's commitment to His own name.

Terrified, they beg Samuel to pray for them. He answers, 'Fear not... only fear the LORD,' and assures them, 'the LORD will not forsake his people for his great name's sake.' Our security rests not on our worthiness but on God's commitment to His own name.

Samuel makes a striking vow: 'God forbid that I should sin against the LORD in ceasing to pray for you.' To stop praying for others is itself a sin. He ends, 'fear the LORD, and serve him in truth with all your heart' — the whole of true religion in a sentence.
